questions.prompt: Why is the ITDR disconnect the step half-done offboardings most often skip?
choices: Once the agents are removed and billing is closed, it feels done.

choices.label: It requires Huntress support to action, so techs forget to file the ticket.
choices.rationale: The disconnect is a portal action the tech runs; it doesn't require a support ticket.

choices.label: It's automated by the bulk-uninstall job, so techs assume it's already done.
choices.rationale: The two are independent and must be disconnected separately.

choices.label: It's only required for M365, not for Workspace.
choices.rationale: Both surfaces need explicit disconnect.

questions.explanation: But the OAuth grant on the customer's tenant is still there, and identity telemetry keeps flowing into the portal for weeks. The disconnect is a single action per tenant; the discipline is doing it, verifying it, and documenting it.

questions.prompt: "Disconnecting one tenant when the customer had both." What's the consequence?
choices: The portal continues to receive telemetry from the remaining connected tenant;

choices.label: Huntress automatically disconnects the second tenant 24 hours later.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour. There's no auto-disconnect cascade.

choices.label: The disconnect on the first tenant reverses itself.
choices.rationale: The first disconnect stands; the issue is the second isn't done.

choices.label: The OAuth grant on the disconnected tenant becomes a security risk.
choices.rationale: The disconnected tenant is fine; the problem is the still-connected one continues to ingest.

questions.explanation: The offboarding looks complete but isn't. A customer with both M365 and Workspace needs both disconnected. Doing one and not the other is the named half-state.

questions.prompt: The runbook calls for "customer-side removal" of the OAuth artefact. What does that mean in practice?
choices: After the portal disconnect, the customer's admin removes the enterprise application.

choices.label: Huntress sends the customer an automated email asking them to remove the app themselves.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour. The MSP coordinates the customer-side step.

choices.label: The customer disconnects the integration from their side, instead of from the Huntress portal.
choices.rationale: The portal disconnect happens first; customer-side removal is the second step.

choices.label: The customer pays a "removal fee" to Huntress.
choices.rationale: No such commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: (M365) or revokes the OAuth grant (Workspace) from their own tenant, so the artefact doesn't linger. Some MSPs treat customer-side removal as part of a clean handover; others leave it to the customer. The runbook decides.

questions.prompt: Mid-offboarding for the customer, who has both M365 and Workspace connected. Senior's note says: "Disconnect both, customer-side removal is part of our standard offboarding." What do you do?

choices.label: Disconnect M365, leave Workspace for a few days "in case the customer needs it."
choices.rationale: The customer is offboarding; you don't keep telemetry flowing in case they want it later.

choices.label: Disconnect both and skip the customer-side removal, the customer is gone, they don't care.
choices.rationale: The senior's note included customer-side removal. Cutting it short is a quiet deviation from the runbook.

questions.explanation: Schedule a short call with the customer's admin to remove the OAuth grants on the customer side, per the senior's note. Portal disconnect on both, plus customer-side removal per the runbook. Loop closed cleanly.

questions.prompt: Next morning after disconnecting both tenants, the portal shows a new ITDR Incident Report against an account at the customer's domain arrived overnight, after both disconnects were confirmed. What do you do?

choices.label: Close as benign, the customer is offboarded.
choices.rationale: The new incident is the signal that something didn't take. Dismissing it without investigation lets the underlying issue continue.

choices: Investigate: was it a state-sync glitch, was the disconnect reverted.

choices.label: Re-run the disconnect on both tenants without investigating.
choices.rationale: You might be solving the right problem, or papering over a state-sync issue that re-disconnect won't fix.

questions.explanation: Or is it a late-arriving event from before the disconnect timestamp? Confirm which, then act. Treat the new incident as a debugging signal, not routine triage. Then act on what the investigation tells you.

questions.prompt: Which is the right verify step the morning after an ITDR disconnect?
choices: Confirm the integration shows "Not connected," last-event timestamp is from before the disconnect.

choices.label: Confirm the customer's super-admin received the disconnect confirmation email.
choices.rationale: Not a Huntress-side check. The portal is the verify surface.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket to confirm the disconnect propagated.
choices.rationale: The portal exposes the state directly.

choices.label: Re-run the disconnect to confirm idempotency.
choices.rationale: Not a real verification approach; re-running can introduce state-sync issues.

questions.explanation: No identity-related Incident Reports for this customer arrived in the last 24 hours. State, timestamp, and absence of new incidents are the three checks. If a new incident did arrive, the disconnect didn't take.

questions.prompt: "Not verifying with a 24-hour check." Why is this called a common mistake?

choices.label: The disconnect doesn't fully complete until 24 hours later.
choices.rationale: The disconnect is immediate; the 24-hour check is verification, not propagation delay.

choices.label: Huntress only sends the disconnect confirmation email 24 hours later.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: The customer-side removal can't be confirmed until the next business day.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

questions.explanation: When they don't, the failure is silent, the portal shows "Not connected" but events continue to arrive. A glance the next day catches this. State-sync glitches are rare but not zero. The only way to detect them is the next-day check.

questions.prompt: The customer's admin can't remove the enterprise application from their tenant, the tenant has restrictions or the original consenting admin has left. What do you do?
choices: Bump, customer-side change management; the senior owns the resolution.

choices.label: Force-remove the application from the Huntress side.
choices.rationale: Not a Huntress-side action. Huntress can't remove what's in someone else's directory.

choices.label: Tell the customer to ignore the artefact; it's harmless after the Huntress-side disconnect.
choices.rationale: Possibly true depending on the runbook, but cutting the senior out of a customer-side change-management decision is wrong.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking them to clean up the customer's tenant.
choices.rationale: Huntress doesn't have access to clean up a customer's own tenant.

questions.explanation: Customer-side change management is the senior's territory.

questions.prompt: The customer says they don't want the app removed from their tenant: "we might need this audit trail." What do you do?
choices: Bump, that's a customer-handover negotiation, not a technical step.

choices.label: Agree on the spot, they're the customer, it's their tenant.
choices.rationale: Out of scope for the tech to make customer-handover concessions unilaterally.

choices.label: Override the customer and remove it anyway.
choices.rationale: Removing an artefact from the customer's tenant against their wishes is well above the helpdesk floor.

choices.label: Document it as a runbook exception.
choices.rationale: The tech doesn't create runbook exceptions; that's the senior's call.

questions.explanation: The customer's wish is a handover decision the senior owns.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson explicitly mention "header strip discipline" before running the disconnect?
choices: Disconnecting ITDR on the wrong customer is the most expensive single-click mistake in the offboarding sequence, you'd cut telemetry for an active customer.

choices.label: The Huntress portal occasionally swaps tenants mid-session.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour. The discipline is about the tech's attention.

choices.label: Header strip strips the headers off the OAuth grant before disconnecting.
choices.rationale: Misreads the metaphor. Header-strip is a cognitive discipline.

choices.label: Huntress requires the customer-name confirmation before allowing disconnect.
choices.rationale: Not a platform-enforced gate.

questions.explanation: Confirming you're in the right customer's organisation is the cheap insurance step.
